title: Briana Gude -Hiring Manager Screen | Design Engineer - Client Operations & GTM

Good morning, Briana.
How are you?
I'm okay.
How are you?
Actually, I don't think it's morning for you.
It is the mid-afternoon over here.
Oh, okay.
So not very early.
Happy afternoon.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
You're calling in for Milana?
Yes, I'm in Atlanta right now.
How's it over there?
Beautiful.
Beautiful.
I, over the last couple of years, have developed allergies.
So...
I'm still getting used to that.
I feel like any time I forget, I like to be outside, so I feel like any time I forget to take allergy medicine, I feel like I developed the flu.
And it's just like, I'm getting over that right now, so I'm a little like below 100%.
But I mean, I love Atlanta.
It's where my family is, it's where I was raised, but I'm just like, where are these allergies coming from?
And it's like intense.
The Poland doing a number on you, huh?
Yeah, like every year, literally just for the last three years.
I'm just like, what's going on?
And it's not even allergy season anymore.
So I don't know why I'm still getting sick.
Yeah.
My lady and my boys, it's both of them, or all three of them are heavily affected too.
I'm thankfully the only one who is allergy free on that end.
But I got a range of other things that I got to deal with.
So unfortunately, everybody's got their thing.
Yeah, it's not that thing, yeah.
Me and all my brothers, we're all on the allergy spectrum.
Allergy, some people have that.
Some of us have that.
I got glasses.
I'm just saying, we're just all over the place.
Yeah, these bodies, right?
What are going to do with it?
Yeah, what can we do?
Especially as everything is changing over time as well with our environment.
Well, Briana, thank you so much for taking the time to chat with me here.
I'm super excited to chat with you.
Your profile really stood out, yes.
Thank you.
Your portfolio looks really awesome.
I think you have a fantastic skill set.
So I would love to talk a little bit more about that.
But before we dive into that, I can give you a little bit more background about who I am, what GrowthX is from my perspective and a little bit about the status quo of design.
After that, we'd love to hear more from you about who you are as a designer, what's important to you.
Perhaps you want to share a couple of anecdotes of how you got to where you are.
And we can take it organically from there.
Does that sound good?
Okay.
Yeah, sounds good.
Awesome.
So let's see.
I have been a design practitioner for well over two decades.
I started out as a graphic designer, moving into interaction design.
They called it at the time, but mostly designing icons, websites, you name it.
Went to school for interaction design.
And after I graduated, I started entrepreneurship pretty much immediately.
I founded my own little design firm.
And that was mostly because I had a hard time working with art director.
a graphic designer.
You know, some of the firms that I worked with.
And, yeah, I started my own little design firm to power these little side projects.
And, yeah, this one side project became a little bit larger and spun that off to become a tech company.
Failed forward as a CEO a little bit there.
And ultimately got my second company acquired by a company in San Francisco, in Silicon Valley, Mapbox.
After that, I went over to or I moved to the Bay Area to, yeah, to learn as much as possible and become, you know, it's like a good operator.
And I led a range of design and product orgs since then.
So I was responsible.
Hello for Honor, which is a company that's doing senior care in the U.S.
we think essentially it's like a redesign senior care from scratch there.
Grew the team to about 30, 40 folks.
And then I moved to Lyft, where I led the consumer division.

So took them from a ride share service, taking it from point A to point B by car, to a multimodal transportation service with bikes and scooters and public transit and car rentals, a whole bunch of new entry points.
After Lyft, I went to Grammarly.
At Grammarly, I built up the first iteration of the design org and design culture over there.
So adopted about five designers and hired close to 80 folks over the course of two years there through the pandemic and really helped Grammarly become more of a AI assistant rather than the...
And then I went to Pitch, the presentation software, was VP of product design and research there for a while as well.
And the past couple of years, I've mostly been doing angel investing and, yeah, a little bit of consulting and coaching here and there, I mean, in venture capital.
This past year, I've been really feeling the itch to get back and get my hands dirty and catch the AI wave.
I have found a really amazing team and a set of founders that's working on some things truly unique.
So Daniel and Marcel, the CTO and CEO of the company, respectively, pulled me in pretty early.
And, yeah, growthx is...
I mean, it's like, I'm sure Tucker has spoken to you about, you know, it's like the basics already, but also it's like from a Silicon Valley perspective, it's kind of like an odd duck.
And the type of work that we do is really, really interesting, really exciting.
So we have our agency side of the company, we have our build side of the company.
And yeah, both of these form an ecosystem, the agency side of the company informs the type of work that we execute for clients.

And within that spectrum, you know, it's like there's a challenge of doing things fast, but at the same time, like elevating quality as much as we can, not using AI as a means to an end, but as an enhancer of quality, rather than a replacement of human beings.
Then on the build side, you know, we're observing.
Everything that's happening on the client side and building software to help us do everything a lot better and at much higher quality.
So that's the essence of what we're doing at GrowthX.
Everything is still extremely nascent.

So within the agency side of things, we're still figuring out, okay, what's the best operational model or the best type of people to hire for these new hybrid type roles that use a lot of AI, but at the same time incorporate a lot of the more traditional skills expected in marketing, but also require a lot of agency and, I would say, autonomy to a certain extent.
And on the build side, I was like, what is the right thing to build and when?
So everything is still very much in flux.
It's a startup, super early stage.
Now, when it comes to the design org, that's where things are really exciting.
So Daniel, our CTO, used to be a designer as well.
Marcel, our CEO, used to be in design.
So there's already an understanding of, hey, is a critical component to how we do our business, and we need to invest in that as well.
So the design team currently consists of marketing designers and design engineers.
Design engineers are people that generally have a T-shaped skill set.
And by that, I mean they're as aesthetic as they are versed in code, but at the same time, like understand how both sides of that spectrum influence each other.
And how the combination of the two truly allows these folks to get to realistic solutions in time, which is really fantastic.
Then we have marketing design and marketing designers who focus predominantly on aesthetics, motion, and workflows.
And essentially it's like coming up with workflows to do the more repetitive actions within the work automatically.
And yeah, this is one of the areas where we're trying to see where Atlas or software fits in amongst other areas, of course.
Now, to accelerate a little bit towards the role and what we're looking for here and what the potential challenges are, the role...
It's for client ops and go-to-market.
It's one of our most exciting areas.
What you'll be working on here is a combination of beautiful landing pages that can either be informed by existing brand guidelines.
Some of our clients will have gorgeous brand books, and things are already well-defined, that we can take and move fast on.
Many of them, however, will not have reached that brand maturity, and we will need to step in and help guide them, help find something appropriate.
And the combination of those two things makes the nature of the assignments really diverse from the get-go.
So that's on landing pages.
Growthx, however, we tend to do things a little bit-
It's out of the box for clients as well.
So we tend to think about how can we turn each of our clients into a publisher, into, you know, it's like a channel.
So a lot of the concepts that we come up with for clients will start as Vibe-coded prototypes, for example.
And these could be, an example of this is codelibrary.ai, for example.
This is a, yeah, almost like a small product, micro product that we've built.
For a client that's building up, yeah, value of a different kind than, you know, it's like your, your diamond doesn't landing page does, for example.

So, so then there's those, in addition to that, we do quite a bit of programmatic SEO and landing pages, which require, you know, like high quality templates that we can move a lot of varying copy and imagery into.
So, so, so, so I've spent quite.

I had a bit of time as an IC in the past couple of months doing a lot of these things myself to get to an understanding of, which fidelity do we need to get to, where can we automate, where can we improve overall processes, where do we need to elevate quality overall.
And, yeah, I think it's like on that side, there's quite a bit of, quite a bit of impact that we can, that we can drive as well.

Folks that the person in this role would be working with directly are, you folks in marketing design, go to market, more back-end oriented engineers within, within client ops and the build, the build area.
So, depending on, you know, like the type of client, will be like direct interaction with, with the client, with the client as well.
And let me see.
Yeah, let me actually pause there.
You know, it's like I've been talking for quite some time.
I'm sure you have a million questions as well.
So I would love to give the word to you from here.
Yeah, I mean, I am just like, I'm sorry.
I guess one of the questions that I asked the other day when I was talking to Tucker is like, but what, is there like a specific idea of like a person that you're looking for to fill this role?
I looked at your LinkedIn the other day and I saw that, well, yesterday I noticed, I that you, you know, you just hired a few people.
So I'm wondering, like, are you still building out this team?
Would I or whoever gets this role be the last hire?
Like, like what kind of person are you looking for to mesh into the team that you have built or like are building?
So yeah, the way that we're looking at this is that this will be the person owning this area specifically.
We're not looking to hire that many folks more within engineering product and design at least for the next year or so or until the next raise.
This is an area that has a long growth path as well.
So it's an area that is constantly scaling.
It is a role that's already supported by agencies, for example.
So it's definitely not the type of, this is not going to be, like a, I mean, it's like there will be moments where there will be high volumes of work, but it's not the.

The type of exercise where everything needs to be done, it is more important that we find smart ways to prioritize the work and deliver value in the right places, then it's important to get everything checked off.
So we expect everybody that we hire to take a very critical approach to their role and see, hey, what do I see going well?
Where do I see challenges and opportunities?
And to proactively bring those up and try to tackle those with the team.
So there's plenty of autonomy in terms of figuring out how to shape this moving forward, of course, in partnership with me and the rest of the org.
But you'll be part of the EPD team.
Okay, nice.
Yeah, that makes a lot of sense.
So what is important to you, Briana?
What are you looking for?
I guess I could start by giving you a bit of my background.

I feel like, I mean, my experience does not span the same as yours, but I feel like just hearing you speak about what has prompted you to just make different changes and moves in your career, like, seem quite similar to me and things that I have done to grow and pivot in mind.
So I'm like really excited.
I was really excited hearing about your experience and even about the opportunity to work with you.
But anyways, so I'll just take it back to the beginning.
I graduated college with...
with...
A marketing degree, quickly realized I didn't want to do that.
And so I just like, I was like, you know what?
I really want to make websites.
Like I wanted to do something creative, but I also am like a super technical person.
I loved math and like science and things.
So I took a course.
Have you ever heard of Super High?
I think so.
Yeah, I took, I worked like really closely with the founder of Super High.
I was like, I think like around the time they first launched, that's how I learned how to code.
And just thinking of the background, they teach from a creative perspective.
So it was perfect for me.
It's mostly self-taught, self-whatever, but anyways.
So I learned how to code and then I started freelancing just to be able to get like my ideas out there, learn how to work with people.
And then I got my first like big girl job.
And there I was hired as a solutions engineer, but it was a startup.
So.
So.
So.
It wasn't a lot of people I got to work with.
We were building landing pages for startups like Hubble or Parade, Baboon, Hydrant.

We had a lot of different really fun clients that I got to work with and just kind of learning how to build quickly, but also creatively, but also with the team in mind, but then also with the end user in mind.
Like we started with Ruby.
I helped us move to Next because Ruby didn't make sense for what we were doing.
And so it was just really fun.
And then since it was a small team, I got to grow and like iterate and all those things really quickly.
And then I decided like, okay, I still want to do creative things.
So I ended up working with our head of design and I was like, hey, like, is there any way that I can do both things?
Of course, it's a startup.
So he's like, yeah, sure.
So.
So.
that's how I learned, like, I already have, like, my creative eye, like, my aesthetic and everything from freelancing and just from that type of person.

But there I learned more about, like, having processes as a designer, consistent, like, spacing, like, just all of the conventions that it takes to be a designer that's, like, not just making pretty things.

And because we were doing landing pages, was always, like, now, like, I'm owning design and or development for, like, 10 to 20 clients at a time and working with, like, our marketers on, like, maybe they would give me some copy and say, hey, I need you to make a page for this client.
And we're looking to hit, we're looking to increase conversion rates or whatever the case.
So that job really taught me a lot about, like, like I said, just how to design, how to, how to design with, like.
So I sort of impact in mind, not just making like cute things, but also like, you know, building things that just make sense.
And so I worked there for a few years, like just moving around, learning, learning and painting.

And then, but I always kind of freelanced a little in the background because like my job got my, it like taught me a lot and it gave me a lot of structure, but I still would get random clients that, I mean, big to me, they were like big, like nonprofits and things.
But it would be like, instead of me designing for like some sort of metric, it would be more like community-based design.
So like I would get, I worked with like Hype Club.
I just did a project with HBCUs outside and I did another one.
I can't remember what they were, but it's like always these.

Like, TOC women-led, like, non-profits that have, like, very deep roots in community and, like, they have a very, very, like, I don't, like, the communities are just, just known for something or, like, their style is, like, just very, I'm sorry, I'm losing my words because I'm, I'm.
No, worry, don't worry, don't worry.
They're doing, they're things that are so much bigger than that, than dollars, right?
Yeah, like, they're doing things that, like, they're not even worried about the numbers because, like, the way that they've built their community is just, like, so impactful.
And so that has been really fun.
And that, and how I was saying earlier, like, at work, we were building, you know, for numbers and dollars, but there we're building to kind of create, like, a digital.
I guess home for their communities, like, and it was always really fun because like their experience, like the experience of designing with them is like, how is this going to make you feel?
It got me back to designing things because I think it looks cool or designing things or like exploring just my own style and how to mix it with whatever client I have.
Like, and even like while doing the work, I never really realized that I had my own distinct style.

Like all of my, you know, after looking at all my projects, I'm like, they all kind of look not the same, but I feel like through that, I learned how to like extract what a client is like looking for or like what their style might be, but then put my own kind of spin on it.

And it just always flourished and like became a really cool, beautiful thing that lived on the internet that felt really cool and exciting to me, but also really inclusive for like everyone that worked with them or that was you
Part of their organization or want it to be.
then they were just doing such amazing things.

So I say that to say, I guess throughout my career, I've just had a really good balance of like technicality and like learning why you do certain things, why you design a certain way, why you build a certain way.
But then also learning to have compassion in design and like in even like thinking about the end user when I'm building things.
And so my career has more so what my big girl job was like my only job.
I was laid off last year and then I just started freelancing from there.
And like I built a community of other freelancers and we kind of launched a studio last year.
we've just been kind of taking on projects that feel really aligned with like who we are and what we want to be doing and things like that.
And so I was telling Tucker the other day, I think he asked me what spectrum I feel like I'm more on.
And I think he what spectrum
Design to engineering.
I was just like, honestly, I feel like it depends on the problem that I'm solving for in the moment.
Like, I love design, but I also love engineering.

Like, every few months, I feel like I have a new thing that I've focused on where before I was, like, really diving into the technicality and, like, the meaning behind the designs that I'm making and, like, who I'm designing for.
But then earlier this year, I got really interested in, like, figuring out what's my tech stack and, like, getting really into, like, the technicality of building with Next and TypeScript.
And, you know, I finally gave in to Tailwind and, like, understanding just, like, how to build things in a more thoughtful way, especially being that when I'm freelancing, I'm the only engineer.
So, it's very easy to build things that are, like, a bit messy or that may be a little broken and hacky.
So, I feel like I really tried earlier this year to really dive more into just...
Just...
Just...
Just...
How to have more empathy in, like, the way that I build and the end user and, like, how they're going to experience a site.
And then also, like, diving into CMS is, like, how is my client going to work with this site?
Because, you know, I build headless e-commerce.
So we're working with Shopify and things like that.
And a lot of my clients throughout my freelancing journey have, like, I started building Liquid themes.
And a lot of my clients would be like, I just don't understand Shopify.

So I switched, I bounced around different CMSs, like, try to figure out, you know, what makes it easier for you guys to even be able to, you know, me to hand this off to you and you don't have to keep that way back to me and all these things.
So I was working on that earlier this year.
And then now I'm kind of like, I got into the vibe coding, like, AI kind of thing.
And now I'm like, okay, well, what else can I do with AI?
Like, I mean, vibe coding is cool, but a lot of times it gets really buggy and then you have to figure out, like, all those things.
But like, how can I get deeper?
How can I deeper?
Are there specific areas that you want to learn?
What skills do you want to learn?
What's important for you the next couple of years?
Let's say you land a job.
Where do you want to end up?
Knowing who you are right now as a design engineer, the next, let's say, is like two to four years.
What do want to learn?
Yeah.
I think in the next couple of years, I really want to become a better designer, but like more efficient.
I want like, I mean, not even just designer, but like, I do want to become a really great designer.
I think that's one thing I want to dive into, but I really want to just become more efficient in a way.

I feel like one thing I'm always thinking about, whether it's design or engineering and a reason why I've kind of been like, oh, maybe I should dive a bit deeper into like the things I can do with AIs that I want.
I just want to become more efficient.
Like, I want to be able to build things in a better way, design things in a better way with everyone that's going to be using it in mind.
And just kind of, that's kind of been my thing this year, is figuring that out.
With whatever tech stack I choose, whatever team I'm on.

And, yeah, so I think, like, in a new role, I want to continue to explore my self-creatively, creatively, but then, yeah, just growing as a designer and engineer and being, you know, learning to be even more thoughtful, learning to be even more, you know, maybe faster or whatever the case may be.
I just want to be, I think the word of the day for me is, like, becoming all around a bit more efficient.
But, yeah, I think if I had to pick a skill to grow in, I think design.
Like, that's really why I got into this space in the first place.
I really do want to grow to be a better designer.
Some more thoughts on the designer, feel better things.
Are there any things that you struggle with?
Yeah, I feel like, where do I struggle the most?
Yeah, I feel like there's a lot of things that I struggle with.

But I think whenever I run into an issue or something that I don't know, like for instance, earlier this year, I ran into, well, maybe more so later last year, but I was running into, from the dev side, issues with, what was it?
I built a project with Sanity.
I never really used Sanity before.
And the way that I built it, it was like my client was making updates, but they weren't being pushed live.
I just, I can't remember exactly what the issue was now that up.

But I guess to make the long story shorter, whenever I run into something that is a problem, I tend to deep dive into whatever that is and like go down like YouTube rabbit holes or whatever the case may be to figure out how like the next time I build or the next time I design to not run into that.
But yeah, I think I find myself every time I do something new, there's always something that I run into.
Are you someone that asks for help?
Yeah, I ask for help, especially in my last role.
If it's something that I'm just like, I really don't know what to do here.
Like I try not to spend too much time like figuring it out myself.
I think that was an issue that I used to have, especially when I first started because I felt a little bit of, I don't know, I think because I was self-taught, I felt a little like everything.
Everyone just gets it.
Everyone did boot camps and went to college for this.

So I would try to figure it out myself, but I quickly realized like it takes you much longer to sit and try to figure it out than it does to just ask someone because someone will either let you know or point you in the right direction.
I think now that I'm kind of like freelancing, I'm not really in a role.
don't really have that many people to reach out to.
So that's kind of why I just go down my little deep dives.
But even then, I do have a community of like, I'm really close with the head of design and head of engineering in my last role.
So sometimes when I run into things or sometimes we'll have a check-in and I'll be like, have you ever done this before?
Have you run into this before?
Like, can I bounce ideas off of you?
Yeah, I definitely ask if it's something that I'm just like, I really don't know what's going on here or like, what's the best practice here?
Because a lot of times, like if you ask Chagibati or if you Google, like you get a lot of different responses.
You get a lot of different ideas of what you can do.
And I feel like, you know, asking someone.
Someone that I trust and who understands like a context of what I might be doing better than like Google might, usually give me, you know, put me in a better direction.
Awesome.
Excellent.
Briana, I'm okay to go a couple of minutes over if you have, if you have more questions for me.
Oh, amazing.
I have a couple of questions that I have in town.
So, how are you guys using AI within like the design and build process, like on the team, on this team?
I mean, yeah, pretty much, pretty much everywhere.

It's like, I don't really, I know of a day where we're not using either ChatGPT or Claude or Cursor or Lovable or Bolt, or it's like a combination of all of them and there's also like no correct way of using.
It's like everybody, including myself, I've been designing for over 20 years, all of the rules are kind of like thrown out the window with these new tools, and literally, it's like at all levels.
So the way that we use AI is as creatively as possible.
And I was like, we use it in, we use it operationally, so in how we execute our work, right?
And this is a very personal approach, right?
It's like the way that somebody uses Figma, one designer uses Figma is different from the other way.
Another designer uses Figma, for example.
So when it comes to individual usage, you know, it's like, it's a free-for-all.
That being said, we do have, you know, like specific type of work, especially on the agents.
The side that we do, that are, that is content production, that we try to find standardized methods for, right?
It's like, and then we try to, it's like document and templatize, like, hey, you know, it's like, this is what a good prompt looks like, for example.
This is how you get this type of copy for this type of client, for this type of, for this type of asset.
And that's where it becomes, you know, it's like, a little bit more important to document the actual, the actual steps.
And to, to think about, all right, you know, it's like, this is what I'm creating right now.
How did I actually, how did I actually get there?
It's a lot of, to give you an example, we just onboarded our first, our first marketing, our first marketing designer.
And one of the things that I kept, that I keep telling her is, you know, was like, there's a ton of folks already doing design throughout the org.
And many of them don't have the title of design, never were taught as designers, but that doesn't make them any less of a designer.
They just have less context, less experience, but that means that you can work.
It's not the intent here that we replace the work that they are doing.
We have to art direct them, help them get to better output.

Help them, let them know, like, hey, if you add this and this and this to your prompt, maybe it's like refer to a grid system or some alignment or it's like use vocabulary that generally is like designers or engineers that have a little bit of experience have.
And all of a sudden you are designing through others and scaling your actual skills instead of trying to dive into each problem individually and apply your individual process to that.
So that's...
How we tend to then use AI at scale to help align folks more.
Does that help?
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Yeah, that definitely helps.
I wasn't 100% sure.
And then I was also really curious, what drew you to my candidacy?
I know earlier you were like, I was really excited about your portfolio.
And I'm like, me?
I don't understand why.
Nice.
Your portfolio just looks good.
The details are there.
It shows that you are both a designer and an engineer.
can just, Briana's like, I've reviewed thousands and thousands and thousands of portfolios throughout my career.
And at some point, it's like, you tend to get a feel with...
In the first couple of seconds, know, I like, what type of designer is behind it?
And it is clear that you have an opinionated aesthetic.
You pay attention to details.
And that's very apparent from your portfolio.
In addition to that, you have, you know, work on there that goes a bit beyond, you know, it's like just a run-of-the-mill performance work.
And other commercial landing pages that we've seen.

I think that that type of, those types of, the type of, the level of empathy and listening skills required to get to high-quality results for clients, like that, for example, is exactly the right type of empathy and listening skills.
Thank you.
Like a designer from here to here in terms of quality output.
So that is something that has stood out with you, and that's why we wanted to talk to you.
Oh, amazing.
Yeah, I feel like my portfolio is such a living thing.
It's changed so much over the course of my career, and I just redid it maybe a month ago.
But I tried to build it from the person, not like from the perspective, but like with whoever's going to be looking at it in mind versus before I used to just make something I thought was pretty.
And this particular one, I felt a lot, like it just was a lot more intentional in the way that I built it, the way that I designed it, the things that I put into it.
And so, yeah, I just was curious.
I've been, I always said, I'm always curious as to like what people think about, like how people feel when they think.
It shows.
shows.
I mean, it's like making a portfolio is something that I loathe for myself.
It's very hard to put that together.
And I think it's a difficult thing.
I've never heard a designer that enjoys the practice of putting together their own portfolio.
It always ends up being, you know, it's like a never ending task of never being complete.
Right.
And that's like why we're all designers in the end, why we do this work and why we're getting paid to do that is to worry about other people's imperfections and not about our own.
So, so yeah, it's, it is a very difficult, very difficult exercise.
But yeah, what you did is probably it's like the right thing, the right thing to do.
And yeah, the only thing that I can advise you on that front is the more opinion you can put in it.
like the more, the more you can.
And, you know, like, really make sure that it reflects who you are and how you think about everything, the better there.
Yeah, yeah.
And I think that that speaks to another issue that I have sometimes.

It's like, especially when I'm designing for myself or if I'm designing something that I really, really care about, I can get into those, like, endless iteration loops because I'm just like, this can be a little bit better.
It's not quite there.
And I feel like when I get into that, I have to remind myself, like, okay, version one or, like, this version doesn't have to be absolutely perfect.
Like, you can always, you know, come back later.
You just have to put it out there.
And so that's something I always have to tell myself, especially, like, when I'm designing for myself or for a client or whatever the case.
Like, a lot of times, especially if I love the aesthetic of something that I'm building, it's, like, very difficult sometimes to just close the lid.
I know, but it's also hard to let go of that.
I've often described it with other folks as you're chasing a specific type of energy or emotion, and it's very ephemeral, ultimately, right?
So that's the difficulty about doing this type of work, too.
It's like you get into some kind of hyper-focus mode that you really got to chase.
Getting into that creativity is also not a given, right?
It's like it takes a while to get to that point, to let everything marinate until the stuff actually comes out.
And when it comes out, you prefer it not to.
Not to.
Yeah, but don't stop me, either, because when I stop, I won't start up again.
it'll take a long time to get back there.
So it's highly challenging.
You picked a difficult career, Briana.
Yeah, I did, but I love it.
I really do love it.
I just had a question.
Oh, I know that the company is remote first and you guys are like everywhere.
How do you stay connected or like stay kind of on the same page as a team?
Yeah.
So we just have the four hours overlap with SF.
Okay.
Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah.
That is where the majority of, you know, like real-time sync takes place.
And that happens like we're doing right now.
Slack is where everybody lives.
So the majority of communication happens over Slack.
And I was like, it is that I'm hiring right now that email traffic has picked up again.
But before that, you know, it's like there, there's no email whatsoever.
definitely friendswhole like Like, in fact, guess don't No, Which
We keep track of tasks and linear, that's where we do the majority of our project management, and then everything that becomes source of truth, we move that into Notion.
And that is super well-documented, so onboarding with us is like, hey, just spend the week in Notion, reading everything, drinking from the fire hose, watching hundreds of videos.
That's kind of, it's like how we drive communications.
We have a bi-weekly all-hands, where you bring the whole company together.
We do, let's see, or at least like I do weekly, weekly one-on-one check-ins with all my directs.
We have a bi-weekly design sync as well, which is essentially like an hour where the designers get together and chat about.

We everything and nothing, this can be used as impromptu crit or to just discuss designy things, and let's see, what did I forget, we tend to come together physically in San Francisco, or at least the plan is to do that every quarter or so, but I've been going now almost every two months.
There is, but that's more of like the leadership interaction there.
When we get together in San Francisco, there's like a full agenda and activities, and it's nice and structured, and let's see, am I missing anything?
I think that's about it when it comes to rituals and interaction.
Okay, cool.
Well, yeah, I think the majority of the lingering questions that I had, you have already answered.
One thing I will say, I think, in my call the other day with Tucker, he mentioned that, you know, like the service side and like the product side.
I can't remember if this role touches both.
remember you saying like, internally, you're building products that you then use to offer services.
Yeah, it's a little bit complicated.
Like from a reporting perspective and from a reporting perspective and the core team that you're part of, you're part of the EPD team.
So part of the software team.
Officially, but as part of that software team, we have a team that we call, sorry, that we call Client Ops, and that team exclusively works with the Deliver team.

So they spend less time contributing directly to the software that we're building or the platform that we're building, and they spend more time building custom backends for clients, for example, or shipping the landing pages or comparative pages, these types of things.
And also, I forget, build and activate the workflows that help us speed up that work in the software as well.
That's what the Client Ops team does, and within Client Ops, yeah, we just hire a...
A couple of product engineers, so these will be more full-stack engineers that this design engineer would be working with on a day-to-day basis.
Okay, that makes so much sense.
Sorry, I've been taking notes as you've been talking.
Well, yeah, I'm really excited.

I think I feel like the role, one, have a lot more clarity into what the role is and the team and everything, but it feels very aligned with things I have been doing, things that I'm, like, quite interested in.
I really appreciate your, like, approach and, like, thoughtfulness when it comes to design.
Like, can tell in just the way that we've spoken, I feel like that in itself is not always there, especially in.
Like, you know, companies and things, I know you mentioned earlier that everyone has a bit of a sad background, so everybody really understands that.
And I'm like, ooh, that's really exciting, because I haven't really experienced that.
Yeah, I'm excited.
I feel like this was a really good conversation.
I'm happy that I got to talk to you today.
Likewise, Briana.
Yeah, yeah.
I'm going to sink back with Tucker, and you'll hear from us.
And also, please add me on LinkedIn, and I would love to stay connected with you, regardless.
Okay, okay.
Amazing.
will do.
Excellent.
Looking forward to seeing you again.
Yeah, enjoy the rest of your day.
Cheers.
Bye-bye.
Bye.

## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager screen for Design Engineer role in Client Operations & GTM at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=2.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX is a unique Silicon Valley startup with agency and build sides, focusing on AI-enhanced quality in marketing and software development](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
  - [The role involves creating landing pages, vibe-coded prototypes, and programmatic SEO templates, balancing aesthetics and code](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
  - [Briana's diverse background in design and engineering, coupled with her empathetic approach to client work, aligns well with GrowthX's needs](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=1035.0)
  - [GrowthX uses AI extensively in their workflow, encouraging creative and efficient use of tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and others](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=1957.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX Company Overview

  - [Dual-sided business: agency and build sides](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=415.0)
  - [Agency side informs client work, build side develops software to enhance operations](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=441.0)
  - [Early-stage startup, still defining operational models and product direction](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=537.0)
  - [Strong design focus due to leadership's design background](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=551.0)
  - [Design team includes marketing designers and design engineers](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=570.0)

### Role Description and Expectations

  - [Position: Design Engineer for Client Ops and Go-to-Market](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
  - [Tasks: Creating landing pages, vibe-coded prototypes, programmatic SEO templates](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=712.0)
  - [Requires balance of aesthetic skills and coding abilities](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=587.0)
  - [Emphasis on finding efficient solutions and prioritizing work effectively](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=901.0)
  - [Opportunity for growth and shaping the role's future direction](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=901.0)

### Briana's Background and Skills

  - [Diverse experience in marketing, web development, and design](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=1035.0)
  - [Self-taught coder with a creative background](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=1088.0)
  - [Experience in both technical and community-focused projects](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=1122.0)
  - [Strong interest in efficiency and continuous learning](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=1644.0)
  - [Desire to grow further as a designer while maintaining technical skills](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=1727.0)

### AI Integration in GrowthX Workflow

  - [Extensive use of AI tools (ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, Lovable, Bolt)](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=1957.0)
  - [Encouragement of creative AI usage for individual work processes](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=1957.0)
  - [Standardization of AI prompts for specific content production tasks](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=1957.0)
  - [Focus on using AI to scale design skills and guide non-designers](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=1957.0)

### Remote Work Culture at GrowthX

  - [Quarterly in-person gatherings in San Francisco](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=2651.0)
  - [Comprehensive onboarding process using Notion documentation](https://fathom.video/share/f3o2yvu6sBS63q_-4uMWFtGNvGKwBSak?tab=summary&timestamp=2596.0)
